plainMeaning: Hail to the Saṅgha, which is like a spotless full moon: its deer-mark is austerity and self-restraint, its cool light is pure right-faith, and the 'Rāhu' of wrong belief can never swallow it.
recitation: tava saṁjama miya laṁchaṇa, akiriya rāhu muha duddharisa niccaṁ | jaya saṁgha canda nimmala, sammatta visuddha juṇhāgā ||

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. tava < Skt. tapas, 'heat, ascetic practice'; here first member of the compound tava-saṁjama.
wordByWord.notes: A glossary term; here it forms the moon's 'deer-mark' jointly with saṁjama.
Paired with tapa as the moon's mṛga-lāñchana.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. miya < Skt. mṛga, 'deer'; alludes to the deer-shaped shadow on the moon's disc.

wordByWord.notes: Poetic name for the lunar blemish; in Indian convention the moon's shadow is read as a deer.

wordByWord.gloss: the doctrine of non-action / wrong belief (akriyā)
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. akiriya < Skt. akriyā, a-(not) + kriyā (action); the heretical akriyāvāda that denies karmic consequence.
wordByWord.notes: Stands in apposition to rāhu: the 'Rāhu' here IS akriyāvāda, the false view.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. rāhu, the seizer who swallows sun and moon to cause eclipses.
wordByWord.notes: The standard antagonist of the moon; here identified with wrong doctrine.
Rāhu-muha = 'the maw of Rāhu', the devouring jaws that the Saṅgha-moon resists.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. duddharisa < Skt. durdharṣa, dur-(hard) + √dhṛṣ (to assail); 'impossible to overpower'.
wordByWord.notes: Predicated of the Saṅgha-moon vis-à-vis Rāhu's maw.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. √ji, 'to conquer'; jaya as a benedictory acclamation.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the second half as a shout of triumph to the Saṅgha-moon.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṁ-√han, 'that which is held together'.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; subject of the whole simile.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. canda < Skt. candra, 'the shining one'.
wordByWord.notes: saṅgha-canda: the governing image of this verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. nimmala < Skt. nirmala, nir-(without) + mala (impurity).
wordByWord.notes: A deliberate paradox with laṁchaṇa: the moon bears a 'mark', yet is 'spotless' — its only 'blemish' is virtue itself.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. sammatta < Skt. samyaktva, 'rightness of insight'.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; here the substance of the moonlight.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. visuddha < Skt. viśuddha, vi-(thoroughly) + √śudh (to purify).
Pkt. juṇhā < Skt. jyotsnā, 'moonlight'; the final -gā is a metrical/dialectal extension of juṇhā.
wordByWord.notes: Whose moonlight is pure right-faith. The lengthened form juṇhāgā fits the gāthā cadence; see notes for the variant.

literalTranslation: Its deer-mark is austerity-and-restraint; ever unassailable by the maw of Rāhu — that is, of wrong-action (akriyā). Hail to the spotless Saṅgha-moon, whose moonlight is wholly pure right-faith!
idiomaticTranslation: Behold the Saṅgha as a flawless full moon: austerity and self-restraint are the gentle deer-mark upon its face, and pure right-faith is the cool radiance it pours forth — forever beyond the reach of Rāhu's devouring jaws, the dark doctrine of non-action that denies all karmic fruit. Victory, victory to the spotless Moon of the Saṅgha!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having raised the Saṅgha as a lotus in the previous gāthās, the hymn now lifts the gaze to the night sky: the Saṅgha is the full moon (saṅgha-canda). The simile is built on a single elegant paradox. An ordinary moon is 'spotless' (nimmala) only in spite of its deer-mark; this Moon is spotless because of it — for its blemish is nothing but tava-saṁjama, austerity and self-restraint, the very disciplines that purify. What looks like a stain to worldly eyes is, in truth, the mark of perfected monastic life. The poet then names the moon's one true enemy. In Indian lore the moon is endangered only by Rāhu, who swallows it in eclipse; here Rāhu is decoded — its devouring maw (rāhu-muha) is akriyā, the heretical doctrine of non-action that denies the karmic weight of deeds. Yet this Moon is duddharisa niccaṁ, eternally unassailable: no eclipse of false belief can darken a community grounded in right-faith. The second line resolves the image into a cry of triumph (jaya): the cool, life-giving moonlight the Saṅgha sheds upon the world is sammatta-visuddha-juṇhā — moonlight that is itself wholly pure samyaktva. Where the lotus-verses showed the Saṅgha rising untouched above karmic flood-waters, this verse shows it shining untouched above doctrinal darkness; the next gāthā will turn from the cool moon to the blazing sun (saṅgha-sūrya), whose fierce austerity-rays scorch rival 'planets'. Moon and sun together frame the Saṅgha as the complete luminary of the night and the day of the spiritual world.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse compresses two of the three jewels into one picture: cāritra (right-conduct, as tapa-saṁyama, the deer-mark) and samyag-darśana (right-faith, as the moonlight). The chosen adversary is doctrinally precise: akriyāvāda, the wrong view that actions leave no karmic trace, is one of the classic mithyātva positions Mahāvīra refuted. By making this the 'Rāhu', the gāthā affirms the Jain cornerstone that every act of body, speech, and mind binds karma — and that a Saṅgha rooted in samyaktva is structurally immune to its denial.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter, the moon models a quiet confidence: discipline (tapa-saṁyama) need not be hidden as a 'flaw' but worn as one's defining mark, and clarity of right-faith is the steady light one offers others. When doubt or fashionable 'nothing-matters' thinking (today's akriyā) threatens, recall that a faith-grounded community is duddharisa — never eclipsed.

crossReferences.text: Akriyāvāda among the wrong views (mithyātva) refuted by Mahāvīra
The Saṅgha-as-moon and Saṅgha-as-sun similes of the Nandī maṅgala

sources.consultedFor: Pkt. juṇhā = moonlight (Skt. jyotsnā), confirming the gloss of જુણ્હાગા

sources.title: Durdharṣa — Sanskrit definitions
sources.consultedFor: Skt. durdharṣa 'unassailable, invincible' underlying Pkt. duddharisa (દુદ્ઘરિસ)

sources.consultedFor: lāñchana 'mark/spot' and the mṛga-lāñchana (deer-mark) lunar convention for મિય લંછણ

sources.title: Akriyāvāda — doctrine of non-action and its rejection in Jainism
sources.consultedFor: Identifying અકિરિય (akriyā) as the heretical non-action view = the verse's 'Rāhu'

notes: The reading juṇhāgā (જુણ્હાગા) is taken verbatim from the book; the base word is unambiguously juṇhā = jyotsnā 'moonlight', with the final -gā a metrical/dialectal lengthening — other recensions may print juṇhā / juṇhāya. The construction sammatta-visuddha-juṇhāgā is read as a bahuvrīhi describing the saṅgha-canda ('whose moonlight is pure right-faith'). 'akiriya rāhu muha' is read appositionally — the maw of the Rāhu that is akriyā — rather than as two separate evils; this matches the preface's gloss of v.9 ('unassailable by the Rāhu of wrong action'). Wisdomlib URLs are aggregator entries citing standard lexica; confirm precise wording against Sheth's Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo or Ratnachandra's Ardha-Māgadhī Dictionary where exactness is required.
